zh-hans.lifeSummary: 当John Fraser Of Touchfraser And Cowie于1317年出生在英国苏格兰斯林史爾时，他的父亲Alexander Fraser41岁，母亲Mary de Brus38岁。他和Mary Bisset, Heiress Of The Lands Of Aboyne至少有1个女儿。他去世于大约1335年享年19岁。

nameMeanings.name: Fraser
nameMeanings.attribution: Dictionary of American Family Names © Patrick Hanks 2003, 2006.
meanings: Scottish: apparently a nickname from Anglo-Norman French fraser(e), fresere ‘strawberry plant’. Fresel and Frisel, from Anglo-Norman French fresel ‘strawberry’, are early variants of the Fraser surname, the modern Gaelicized form of which is Friseal. See Frizzell . The crest on the Scottish family's coat of arms takes the form of a strawberry plant, but its antiquity is unknown. Claims of a habitational derivation, in particular from a place called la Frézelière in Anjou (France), are attractive but they lack verifiable evidence.
Americanized form of one or more similar (like-sounding) Jewish surnames.

History: There are two clans Fraser in Scotland, with common ancestry going back to Simon Fraser of Keith in East Lothian, who lived in the 12th century. One of these clans has its seat at Philorth Castle (subsequently re-named Cairnbulg) on the northeast coast of Scotland. Sir Alexander Fraser, 8th laird of Philorth (c. 1536–1623) converted the fishing village of Faithlie into the burgh of Fraserburgh in the 1590s. The other clan is Fraser of Lovat, associated mainly with the city of Inverness are. They are descended from Simon Fraser, a younger son, who lived in the early 14th century. In Gaelic, the head of Clan Fraser of Lovat is known as Mac Shimi ‘son of Simon’.

variants.name: Frayser
meanings: Probably an altered form of   Fraser  .

variants.name: Frasier
meanings: Scottish and northern Irish: variant of   Fraser  .

variants.name: Frasure
meanings: Altered form of Scottish   Fraser  .

variants.name: Frazer
meanings: Variant of   Fraser  , associated chiefly with northern Ireland.

variants.name: Fraizer
meanings: Altered form of Scottish   Fraser  .

variants.name: Frashier
meanings: Scottish and northern Irish: variant of   Fraser  .

variants.name: Fraiser
meanings: Altered form of Scottish   Fraser  .

variants.name: Frizzell
meanings: English and Irish: nickname from Old French fresel, frisel ‘lace, ribbon, braid, brooch, frill, ruff’, for someone with an ornate style of dress, or ‘object of little value’ (a diminutive of frese, of ancient Germanic origin).
Scottish: from Anglo-Norman French fresel ‘strawberry’, an alternative form of Fraser .

variants.name: Frazier
meanings: English and Scottish: variant of   Fraser  .
Americanized form of French Frégeau (see Fregeau ).

variants.name: Frasher
meanings: Scottish and northern Irish: variant of   Fraser  .
Possibly an altered form of German Fratzscher (see Frutchey ).

nameMeanings.name: Of
nameMeanings.attribution: Dictionary of American Family Names © Patrick Hanks 2003, 2006.
meanings: German: possibly a metonymic occupational name for a fisherman, from orfe, a species of freshwater fish. Alternatively, a habitational name from Urff near Kassel, formerly Orpha.

variants.name: Orff
meanings: German: variant of   Orf  .

nameMeanings.name: And
nameMeanings.attribution: Dictionary of American Family Names © Patrick Hanks 2003, 2006.
meanings: Some characteristic forenames:  Jewish Hyman, Isidor, Myer.

English, German, and Jewish (Ashkenazic): metonymic occupational name for a maker of hoops and bands, etc., from Middle English band, bond, Middle High German, Middle Low German bant, German Band ‘hoop, metal band, fetter, shackle’, i.e. something used for tying or binding.
English: variant of   Bond  .

variants.name: Bandt
meanings: German: variant of   Band  .

variants.name: Bandstra
meanings: Some characteristic forenames:  Dutch Gerrit, Wouter.
West Frisian: habitational name composed with Band 4 + the suffix -stra from Old Frisian sittera ‘inhabitant of’.

variants.name: Banta
meanings: West Frisian: probably a habitational name for someone from Bant, in the 17th century an island in Friesland, now a village north of Emmeloord in the Noordoostpolder (see   Band   4, compare   Banda   5). This surname is no longer found in the Netherlands.
History: Epke Jacobs Banta arrived in New Netherland in 1659.

variants.name: Bandel
meanings: Some characteristic forenames:  Jewish Zvi, Emanuel, Hyman, Meir.
South German and Jewish (Ashkenazic): from a diminutive of   Band  . See also   Bendel  .
German: in some cases, a variant of Pandel, a pet form of the medieval personal name Pantaleon (see Pantaleo ).

variants.name: Bend
meanings: English (Leicestershire and Nottinghamshire): from Middle English bend ‘band, fetter, chain; ribbon, sash, stripe’, for someone who made, wore, or used such items. See also the Band 1 and compare Bendy 1.

variants.name: Bandler
meanings: German: occupational name for a maker of band (for vessels) or ribbon or lace, from an agent derivative of Middle High German bandel, a diminutive of bant ‘band’ (see Band ).

variants.name: Bandy
meanings: English: perhaps from an unrecorded Middle English bandi, bondi, bendi, derivatives of Middle English and Old English band, bond, bend ‘band, ribbon, stripe’ + Middle English -i, reinforced by Old French bendé ‘striped’, with reference perhaps to clothing ornamented with ribbons or stripes, or to the diagonal band of a heraldic device. Compare Band .
English: alternatively, perhaps from an unrecorded Middle English bandy, bendy ‘curved stick’, used for someone with bent or crooked legs.
Czech and Slovak: from a derivative of banda ‘(gipsy) band’, apparently a nickname for a bandsman. This surname is found among Gypsies.

variants.name: Bond
meanings: English: status name for a peasant farmer or husbandman, Middle English bond(e), bounde, occasionally bande ‘bondman, customary tenant, serf’ (Old English bonda, bunda, reinforced by Old Norse bóndi). The Old Norse word was also in use as a personal name (Old Norse Bóndi, Bondi, Bundi, Bonde, borrowed as late Old English Bonda), and this has given rise to other English and Scandinavian surnames alongside those originating as status names, such as the Middle English personal name Bonde. The status of the peasant farmer fluctuated considerably during the Middle Ages; moreover, the underlying ancient Germanic word is of disputed origin and meaning. Among ancient Germanic peoples who settled to an agricultural life, the term came to signify a farmer holding lands from, and bound by loyalty to, a lord; from this developed the sense of a free landholder as opposed to a serf. In England after the Norman Conquest the word sank in status and became associated with the notion of bound servitude. The name can also be a variant of Band .
Swedish: variant of   Bonde  .
In some cases also an American shortened form of Ukrainian Bondarenko and possibly also of some other surname beginning with Bond-.

nameMeanings.name: Cowie
nameMeanings.attribution: Dictionary of American Family Names © Patrick Hanks 2003, 2006.
meanings: Scottish: habitational name from any of several places, especially one near Stirling, named Cowie, probably from Gaelic colldha, an adjective from coll ‘hazel’.

variants.name: Cowey
meanings: English (southeastern): habitational name from Cowey Green in Essex.
Scottish and northern English: variant of Scottish   Cowie  , found chiefly in County Durham.

nameMeanings.name: John
nameMeanings.attribution: Dictionary of First Names © Patrick Hanks and Flavia Hodges 1990, 2003, 2006.
meanings: Contracted form of Old French Jo(h)anne, from Latin Io(h)anna ( see Joanna ). In England this was the usual feminine form of John from the Middle English period onwards and was extremely popular, but in the 16th and 17th centuries it steadily lost ground to Jane . It was strongly revived in the first part of the 20th century, partly under the influence of George Bernard Shaw's play St Joan ( 1923 ), based on the life of Joan of Arc ( 1412–31 ). Claiming to be guided by the voices of the saints, she persuaded the French dauphin to defy the occupying English forces and have himself crowned, and she led the French army that raised the siege of Orleans in 1429 . The following year she was captured by the Burgundians and sold to the English, and a year later she was burned at the stake for witchcraft at the age of 18 or 19. Her story has captured the imagination of many writers, and she is variously portrayed as a national and political hero, a model of apolitical straightforwardness and honesty, and a religious heroine. She was canonized in 1920 . More recent influences have included the American film actress Joan Crawford ( 1908–77 , born Lucille le Sueur ), the British actress Joan Collins ( b. 1933 ), the American comedienne Joan Rivers ( b. 1933 ), and the West Indian pop singer Joan Armatrading ( b. 1950 ).

en.lifeSummary: When John Fraser Of Touchfraser And Cowie was born in 1317, in Stirlingshire, Scotland, United Kingdom, his father, Alexander Fraser, was 41 and his mother, Mary de Brus, was 38. He had at least 1 daughter with Mary Bisset, Heiress Of The Lands Of Aboyne. He died about 1335, at the age of 19.

fr.lifeSummary: Lorsque John Fraser Of Touchfraser And Cowie est né en 1317, à Stirlingshire, Écosse, Royaume-Uni, son père, Alexander Fraser, avait 41 ans et sa mère, Mary de Brus, avait 38 ans. Il a eu au moins 1 fille avec Mary Bisset, Heiress Of The Lands Of Aboyne. Il est décédé environ 1335, à l’âge de 19 ans.

de.lifeSummary: Als John Fraser Of Touchfraser And Cowie 1317 in Stirlingshire, Schottland, United Kingdom geboren wurde, war sein Vater Alexander Fraser 41 und seine Mutter Mary de Brus 38. Er hatte mit Mary Bisset, Heiress Of The Lands Of Aboyne mindestens 1 Tochter. Er starb um 1335 im Alter von 19 Jahren.

it.lifeSummary: Quando John Fraser Of Touchfraser And Cowie è nato il nel 1317 nel Stirlingshire, Scozia, Regno Unito, il suo padre, Alexander Fraser, aveva 41 anni e il suo madre, Mary de Brus, aveva 38 anni. Lui avuto almeno 1 figlia con Mary Bisset, Heiress Of The Lands Of Aboyne. Morì circa 1335, all'età di 19 anni.
